verses.text: Isaac calls Jacob and blesses him a second time — this time without trickery. He sends him to his mother's family in Paddan-aram to find a wife. Do not take a Canaanite woman. The covenant must marry into itself.

Isaac calls Jacob and blesses him a second time — this time without trickery. He sends him to his mother's family in Paddan-aram to find a wife. Do not take a Canaanite woman. The covenant must marry into itself.

God Almighty bless thee, make thee fruitful, multiply thee. Give thee the blessing of Abraham, that you may inherit the land of your sojourning. The blessing now flows knowingly to the right son. Isaac has accepted what God had said all along.

God Almighty bless thee, make thee fruitful, multiply thee. Give thee the blessing of Abraham, that you may inherit the land of your sojourning. The blessing now flows knowingly to the right son. Isaac has accepted what God had said all along.
Isaac sends Jacob away. He goes to Paddan-aram, to Laban, his mother's brother. The deceiver begins his long apprenticeship under another deceiver.

Esau watches all this. He sees that Canaanite wives displease his father. So he goes to Ishmael's house and adds Mahalath, Ishmael's daughter, to his existing wives. It is a half-effort to please Isaac — using women to manage a relationship problem. The pattern continues into another generation.

Esau watches all this. He sees that Canaanite wives displease his father. So he goes to Ishmael's house and adds Mahalath, Ishmael's daughter, to his existing wives. It is a half-effort to please Isaac — using women to manage a relationship problem. The pattern continues into another generation.

Jacob leaves Beersheba toward Haran. He comes upon a certain place when the sun sets — Genesis names it later, but for now it is just somewhere. He takes a stone for a pillow and lies down. A fugitive on the ground.

Jacob leaves Beersheba toward Haran. He comes upon a certain place when the sun sets — Genesis names it later, but for now it is just somewhere. He takes a stone for a pillow and lies down. A fugitive on the ground.

And he dreams: a ladder set up on the earth, its top reaching to heaven, and the angels of God ascending and descending on it. The two worlds are not as separate as we think. Traffic moves between them.

Above the ladder stands the LORD: I am the LORD God of Abraham your father, and the God of Isaac. The land whereon thou liest, to thee will I give it, and to thy seed. The personal covenant now claims Jacob by name.

Your offspring will be like the dust of the earth, spreading to every direction. In thee and thy seed shall all families of the earth be blessed. The very promise spoken to Abraham is now poured into the deceiver running for his life.

Behold, I am with thee. I will keep thee wherever thou goest, and bring thee back to this land. I will not leave thee until I have done what I have spoken to thee. This is grace meeting a man who hasn't earned a syllable of it.
Jacob wakes: surely the LORD was in this place — and I knew it not. The God of his fathers has been near all along; the fugitive simply hadn't noticed.
He is afraid: how awesome is this place! This is none other than the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven. Holiness is suddenly more vivid than the night sky overhead.
Early in the morning Jacob takes the stone he had used as a pillow, sets it up as a pillar, and pours oil on its top. He marks the spot — sleep-place turned worship-place.
He names the place Bethel — house of God — though the city had been called Luz before. The name he gives sticks; the old name is mostly forgotten. Encounter renames things.

Jacob vows: if God will be with me and keep me on this road, give me bread and clothing, and bring me safely back — then the LORD will be my God, this stone will be God's house, and of all you give me I will give a tenth back. His first prayer is half-deal, half-faith. God will take both and grow them.
